context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Urban-Rural Linkages and Tradeoffs

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of the urban bias thesis in development? Should countries focus more on linkages that strengthen urban or rural development? What's the impact of industrial growth on class formation and urbanization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on urban bias, urban-rural linkages, and industrial growth. I kinda get the topic, but I’m not sure how to frame my main argument yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — let's dig in. Before thinking about framing, what's your initial take on the urban bias thesis itself? Do you think it accurately describes how development has historically worked, or do you have doubts about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning of the assignment and hasn't yet articulated any position. Rather than scaffolding the framing for them, I should use a Socratic opening question to get them to surface their own initial thinking about the urban bias thesis. This avoids over-directing while getting the conversation moving productively toward the core argument they need to develop.

exchanges.student: I think it means states usually favor cities over rural areas, like in spending or policy, but I’m a little fuzzy on the details. My first take is that it seems kinda true historically, but maybe too simple on its own?
exchanges.tutor: That's a promising instinct. What specifically makes you think it might be too simple? Can you think of any cases or dynamics it might be missing or overlooking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuitive grasp of urban bias and is already showing critical thinking by questioning its simplicity. I should validate the direction without confirming or completing the thought, and push them to be more specific — what makes it 'too simple'? This will help them develop a more substantive argument for their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: Yeah, maybe cuz it makes it sound like urban and rural areas are totally separate, when they’re linked a lot. I also feel like it might miss who benefits inside cities or villages, not just city vs countryside.
exchanges.tutor: Those are two sharp observations. How do you think industrial growth fits into this picture? Does it complicate or reinforce the urban bias dynamic you're describing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two genuinely strong critiques of the urban bias thesis: the interconnectedness of urban and rural areas, and the intra-sector inequality dimension. These are both analytically rich points that can anchor their reflection piece. I should affirm the direction and push them to connect these insights to the prompt's other questions — specifically about industrial growth and class formation — to help them see how these pieces fit together into a coherent argument.

exchanges.student: I think it prob does both tbh. Industrial growth can pull investment and jobs into cities, but it also changes rural areas through migration, markets, and like class shifts maybe?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you mentioned 'class shifts.' What do you think actually happens to class structure when industrialization pulls people and resources toward cities? Who gains, who loses, and where?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress connecting industrial growth to both urban concentration and rural transformation. They've touched on migration, markets, and class formation — all relevant to the prompt. Now is a good moment to push them to be more precise about the 'class shifts' idea, since that's explicitly in the prompt and will strengthen their argument if developed.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing factory owners, urban elites, and maybe some formal workers gain first. Rural peasants or informal workers might get left out, but ngl I’m not fully sure how to say the class formation part right.
exchanges.tutor: You're on a reasonable track. Think about this: when rural peasants migrate to cities for factory work, do they simply 'move up,' or does something more complicated happen to their position in society? What might that tell you about how classes form?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuitive grasp of class formation dynamics under industrialization. They're self-aware about their uncertainty. Rather than explaining class formation theory to them, I should ask a guiding question that helps them think through the mechanisms — specifically how migration and labor market shifts reorganize class positions — without giving them the answer. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: Yeah, not just move up I think — they might become wage laborers but still be pretty vulnerable. So maybe industrialization creates a new urban working class instead of just reducing poverty automatically?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight and it directly speaks to the prompt. You now have building blocks covering all three questions — urban bias, urban-rural linkages, and class formation. What do you think your central argument tying these together could be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong insight — that industrialization produces a new urban working class rather than automatically lifting people out of poverty. This is a substantive analytical point that directly addresses the prompt. We've now covered all three prompt questions reasonably well across the conversation. I should affirm this and push them toward thinking about how to tie these ideas together into a coherent argumentative thread for their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: Maybe my argument is that urban bias is real, but it’s not enough by itself, cuz development depends on urban-rural linkages and industrial growth can deepen inequality while also creating new classes. Is that too broad?
exchanges.tutor: It covers the ground, but ask yourself: what's the one core tension or claim you want the reader to walk away with? Right now you have three ideas — can you find the thread that connects them into a single sharper point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has formulated a reasonable central argument that touches on all three prompt questions. It is somewhat broad, but rather than telling them that directly, I should ask them to think about what the core tension or claim is — pushing them to sharpen it themselves. This is a good moment to nudge toward specificity without doing the work for them.

exchanges.student: Hmm idk, maybe the thread is that focusing only on either cities or villages is the problem? Like development works better when policy sees them as connected, since industrial growth reshapes both places and social classes.
exchanges.tutor: That's a much tighter thread — good. You've done enough thinking here to start writing. Try drafting your opening paragraph based on that central argument and share it when you're ready.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a cleaner, more integrated argument. This is good progress. We've now spent several exchanges developing the argument, and the student has enough to start writing. Given the conversation length configuration and the rules about pushing toward written output when there's been sufficient conceptual development, I should now encourage the student to move from discussion to an actual writing attempt.

exchanges.student: Ok yeah, I think my opening could say urban bias explains a lot, but it can be misleading if it treats urban and rural development as opposites. My main point would be that countries should focus on the linkages between them, because industrial growth connects the two while also producing uneven class formation and urbanization.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a solid direction. Now stop describing it and actually write it — put those ideas into your opening paragraph and share the draft here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a solid conceptual direction for their opening. However, per the rules, I should not write, polish, revise, or suggest language for them — they need to actually draft the paragraph themselves. I should affirm the direction and prompt them to write it out, rather than letting them describe what they'll write.

exchanges.student:
Okay, here’s my draft opening paragraph:

The urban bias thesis is useful because it shows how development policy has often favored cities over rural areas through investment, infrastructure, and political attention. At the same time, the thesis is too limited if it treats urban and rural areas as separate spaces with opposing interests. In reality, the two are deeply connected through migration, markets, labor, and state policy. For this reason, countries should not simply choose whether to prioritize urban or rural development, but should focus on the linkages between them. Industrial growth makes this especially clear, because it can drive urbanization and economic change while also creating new class divisions and unequal outcomes across both urban and rural society.
exchanges.tutor: This is a good opening — your argument is clear and it addresses all three parts of the prompt. One thing to watch: you make several claims in that last sentence that will each need to be supported in the body. Which of those claims do you plan to develop first, and what evidence or reasoning will you use to back it up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid opening paragraph that captures their central argument well. It addresses the urban bias thesis, the linkages theme, and industrial growth/class formation — all three elements of the prompt. Per the rules, I should not grade or polish it, but I can give a general evaluative comment and constructive feedback. I should also push them forward to the body of the reflection rather than dwelling on the opening.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently guiding the student through discovery rather than providing answers. All three prompt components (urban bias, urban-rural linkages, class formation) were developed by the student through targeted questioning. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a conclusion or did the reasoning work — each turn posed a question that pushed the student to generate the next idea. The student arrived at the central argument organically by Turn 8, and the tutor's final response correctly pointed to what still needed development without supplying it. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., picking up 'class shifts' in Turn 4, 'wage laborers' in Turn 6) and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback, notably in Turn 7 asking the student to find a single unifying thread, and in Turn 10 flagging that multiple claims would each need evidential support. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed steadily without repetition or off-topic drift. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-targeted, and maintained a supportive coaching tone throughout without implying formal grading.
